update_actions.description: maker_lab_frontage (transparent maker lab): add a realistic visible maker/co-working interior connected to existing building, wall, door, or window edges.
prototype_display (prototype display wall): add one realistic display zone with abstract diagrams, small models, or material samples anchored to a wall/building frontage or public node.
collaboration_seating (modular bench/worktable): add realistic modular seating and a worktable near existing trees, sidewalk, entrance, or public edge.
smart_bench (charging smart bench): add one realistic smart bench with subtle charging details near a public path or shaded edge.
rain_garden (native rain-garden planting): add native planting that links tree/plant nodes to seating and stormwater edge.
micro_mobility_dock (bike/scooter dock): add a compact micro-mobility dock at the street, entrance, or public-space edge without blocking circulation.
community_screen (interactive community screen): add one restrained screen or panel with abstract graphics only, supporting prototype discussion and public information.

facade_depth_system (3D facade/spatial interface): use wall thickness, doorway depth, canopy, window reveal, platform edge, and ground-plane connection to make the update read as a real three-dimensional spatial scene.

color_block_wayfinding (reversible color accents): add restrained colored blocks on paving, movable furniture, display plinths, or privacy-safe panels to clarify activity zones without readable text or changing building mass.
sparse_activity_scene (limited human activity): add a small believable activity scene with no more than 5 people total, such as discussion, prototype viewing, resting, or slow-mobility arrival.
outdoor_collaboration_field (public work/learning zone): add realistic modular tables or benches for small group discussion, outdoor learning, and prototype exchange.
shaded_public_node (tree/canopy relation): strengthen shade with seating, rain-garden planting, and comfortable gathering around existing trees or open edges.
public_demo_micro_stage (prototype demonstration): add a low-key public demonstration/display zone integrated with the plaza or path, not a formal stage.
slow_mobility_threshold (bike/scooter dock and wayfinding): add a compact micro-mobility and wayfinding node that does not block walking space.

public_furniture_palette (diverse street furniture): vary furniture forms according to the public-space background, combining long timber benches, concrete seat blocks, slim steel-frame worktables, planter edges, movable stools, shaded meeting tables, and low information/display plinths.

public_material_coherence (context-specific materials): use durable paving-compatible materials such as light precast concrete, warm timber, brushed or dark steel, perforated metal shade elements, planting beds, and subtle glass/display surfaces that match the existing buildings and open ground.

diverse_street_furniture_materials (function-matched furniture): use varied but coherent street furniture materials and forms matched to the background, circulation, shade, and innovation-community use.

contextual_facade_material_forms (facade material/form diversity): diversify facade or boundary treatment with context-specific materials, depth, openings, screens, shelves, canopies, and planted edges where the existing scene supports them.
vehicle_area (car/vehicle): reduce dominant parked or service vehicles and reconstruct a slow, pedestrian-first innovation-community edge.
closed_boundary (wall/fence): soften the closed boundary and selectively open it as an accessible innovation-community interface.
empty_or_passive_open_space: reduce empty pass-through feeling and avoid over-building the open public space.

- Treat open ground, tree shade, seating, path edge, and public boundary as the main spatial carriers; keep the space open and accessible.
- Prioritize public collaboration: outdoor learning, small prototype demonstration, flexible seating, rain-garden shade, and slow-mobility connection.
- Progressive change level: level03_stronger_but_buildable. Use a stronger but still physically buildable update: add more complete public/work thresholds, richer material and furniture diversity, clearer innovation-community activity scenes, and more visible color-block spatial cues without changing the camera, major massing, or residential use if detected.
- Function-specific focus: public-space innovation community: open civic collaboration, shaded outdoor work/learning, public prototype demonstration, flexible plaza/street-edge activity, and accessible slow-mobility connections; Use a public-space palette tied to the existing site: light/dark concrete paving, timber seating surfaces, powder-coated or galvanized steel frames, modular concrete blocks, integrated planters, permeable paving, rain-garden planting, and subtle glass/metal information panels. Avoid a single-material furniture set.; Street furniture should be varied by function: long benches for waiting, table-bench sets for outdoor work, small round discussion tables, leaning rails, planter-benches, micro-mobility docks, charging posts, and low prototype display plinths. Keep circulation open and distribute furniture along shade, path edges, and building frontage.; When a building frontage is present, use light-touch public interface forms: shallow awnings, window-side work ledges, glass or metal display panels, recessed seating edges, and shaded transition zones. Do not create enclosed new buildings in the public space.; building / facade / door / window / wall relations should become transparent maker-lab frontage, open innovation studio, and prototype display interface; street / sidewalk relations should become a slow public threshold with micro-mobility, collaboration seating, and visible community activity; tree / planting relations should become shaded worktables, smart benches, rain-garden edges, and comfortable discussion spaces; closed boundary relations should become a permeable innovation-community porch, entrance, or display window.
- Material and form diversity: Use a public-space palette tied to the existing site: light/dark concrete paving, timber seating surfaces, powder-coated or galvanized steel frames, modular concrete blocks, integrated planters, permeable paving, rain-garden planting, and subtle glass/metal information panels. Avoid a single-material furniture set.
- Street furniture diversity: Street furniture should be varied by function: long benches for waiting, table-bench sets for outdoor work, small round discussion tables, leaning rails, planter-benches, micro-mobility docks, charging posts, and low prototype display plinths. Keep circulation open and distribute furniture along shade, path edges, and building frontage.
- Facade/boundary form diversity: When a building frontage is present, use light-touch public interface forms: shallow awnings, window-side work ledges, glass or metal display panels, recessed seating edges, and shaded transition zones. Do not create enclosed new buildings in the public space.
- Make the update clearly about learning, making, prototyping, co-working, public demonstration, and community collaboration.
Function-specific avoidances:
- do not overfill the public space with buildings or enclosed rooms
- do not make it look like a decorative park disconnected from innovation-community use

Final constraints:
- Preserve the original camera viewpoint, perspective, major building geometry, street/yard layout, trees, and site identity.
- Keep the result photorealistic and physically buildable as a real street, factory-edge, or public-space scene, not a staged render.
- Make building facade, wall surface, entrance depth, canopy, ground plane, and open-space furniture read as a coherent 3D spatial system where relevant.
- Diversify street furniture materials and forms according to function and background: benches, worktables, leaning rails, planter-benches, micro-mobility docks, display plinths, and charging details should not all look identical.
- Diversify facade and boundary material expression where relevant: preserve existing brick/concrete/steel/glass texture, add selective openings, screens, shelves, canopy depth, planted edges, or display niches; do not apply one flat decorative skin.
- Keep the exact original image viewpoint and framing; do not rotate, zoom, crop differently, or change the camera height/lens feeling.
- Do not add decorative lighting, warm ambient lighting, glowing light strips, or night-scene effects. If lighting is adjusted, only use realistic neutral white daylight/white functional light and minor brightness correction.
- If the scene or AI diagnosis indicates residential building use, preserve the residential function and do not convert it into maker lab, retail, office, or public exhibition; only add light public-edge improvements that do not disturb housing privacy.
- Use refined steel, glass, warm timber, robust paving, native planting, and believable human activity without weakening the real street-scene feeling; people are optional and must be sparse, with no more than 5 people in one image.
- Colored blocks are allowed as restrained, reversible innovation-community cues on paving, furniture, display plinths, privacy-safe screens, or activity-zone markers; do not repaint or alter residential buildings aggressively.
- Activity scenes are allowed when they support the trained pattern update, such as small discussion, prototype viewing, outdoor work, resting, or slow-mobility arrival; keep activity believable and never exceed 5 people.
- Use icon-only wayfinding and abstract display graphics; no readable text, no logos, no fake words.
- Avoid fantasy architecture, generic beautification, excessive cars, empty-plaza feeling, and changes unrelated to innovation-community use.
- Do not obstruct the original function of existing spaces: keep traffic roads, fire lanes, service access, gates, ramps, sidewalks, and main pedestrian paths clear; place furniture, displays, planters, color blocks, people, and bicycle facilities only along safe edges or activity pockets.
